title: Insulating a garden room or workshop with PIR board
subtitle: Floor, walls, and roof to a functional working standard
excerpt: A typical timber-frame garden room or workshop loses most of its heat through the roof, then walls, then floor. Rigid PIR board insulation at 50 to 100 mm in each plane brings the space to a heated-office standard. This covers the installation sequence for all three planes.
sourceNotes: PIR board product data sheets (Celotex, Kingspan TB3000 published specifications). Timber-frame thermal bridging correction factors from BS EN ISO 6946.

glossaryTerms.definition: Polyisocyanurate rigid foam insulation board. Lambda typically 0.022 to 0.023 W/m·K, giving high R-value per mm of thickness.
A membrane placed on the warm side of insulation to limit moisture diffusion into the insulation from the interior.
An alternative term for thermal bridge: a path through the building fabric with higher conductivity than the insulation layer, creating a local cold spot.

content.text: A 3 m by 4 m timber-frame garden room with 50 mm
PIR board

insulation throughout takes a weekend's work. With 75 mm PIR in the walls and 100 mm in the roof, you can hold 18°C with a 1500 W electric panel heater in a 5°C outside temperature, running around 6 to 8 hours per day. The same room with no insulation needs 3000 to 4500 W continuously to hold that temperature.

Before starting: check whether the garden room is a permitted development or has planning permission. If it is used as a habitable room (bedroom, home office overnight sleeping), it needs to comply with Building Regulations. An unheated garden room used only as a hobby space or day office is typically outside the regulations. If in doubt, contact your local authority's planning or Building Control department.
Materials

PIR boards: 75 mm for walls (lambda 0.022, R = 3.4), 100 mm for roof (R = 4.5), 50 mm for floor (R = 2.3). Foil-faced boards are self-finishing on the warm side and act as the vapour control layer if joints are taped.
Foil tape: 50 mm wide aluminium foil tape for all board joints. Butyl-backed foil tape outperforms standard tape for long-term adhesion.
Low-expansion foam: for perimeter gaps between board and timber frame studs. Do not use high-expansion foam, which can distort frames.
Plasterboard or OSB lining: 12.5 mm plasterboard for a decoratable finish, or 18 mm OSB for a workshop that will take fixings and shelving. Fix to the studs through the PIR board with 100 mm screws.
Safety

Before you start cutting or drilling. Eye protection for every cut. Gloves when handling cut PIR edges. PIR board is not classified as hazardous when cutting with a knife (no dust release comparable to mineral wool), but fine particles are released; a dust mask is sensible in an enclosed space.
Floor

Sweep and level the sub-floor. Concrete base: lay a damp-proof membrane (1200-gauge polythene) first if the slab has no integrated DPM. Timber joist floor: the PIR boards fit between joists from above before the floorboard is relaid.
Cut 50 mm PIR to fit tightly between joists or lay full boards on a slab. Score both sides and snap; for tight-fitting between joists, add a light bead of foam at each cut edge to seal.
Refix the floorboard or lay 18 mm OSB on top of the PIR, offset from board joints. Fix with screws into joists at 400 mm centres.
Walls

Measure the stud spacing from the inner face. Most garden rooms have 400 or 600 mm stud centres. Cut PIR to 370 or 570 mm wide respectively for a snug fit, plus full-height pieces to fill the stud cavity.
Slide or press boards between studs. If the stud cavity is shallower than 75 mm, use 50 mm boards between studs and add a 25 mm continuous layer over the studs to eliminate the
cold bridge
at stud timber.
Tape all board joints with foil tape. The foil face of the board acts as a vapour control layer when joints are sealed.

Fix the lining (plasterboard or OSB) through the PIR into the studs with 100 mm screws at 400 mm centres vertically. If adding a continuous PIR layer over the studs, fix with longer screws to reach the stud at the back.
Roof

A shed-roof or apex garden room typically has exposed rafters on the inside. Fit PIR between rafters at full depth (often 75 to 100 mm), then add a 25 mm continuous layer below the rafters to eliminate the rafter
cold bridge

Maintain a 50 mm clear air gap between the top of the PIR and the underside of the roof deck for ventilation, unless using a warm-roof build-up where the PIR is above the deck. A cold-roof installation needs the air gap to prevent condensation on the deck.
Tape all joints. Fit the lining board (12.5 mm plasterboard is standard for fire performance) with 100 mm screws into rafters.
Done well vs done badly

Done well: boards fit snugly in all cavities, all joints taped, a continuous layer over studs and rafters to eliminate cold bridges, lining fixed securely. A heated electric panel runs quietly on a timer and the room is usable year-round at modest running cost.
Done badly: boards loose in cavities with gaps, joints untaped, no continuous layer over the studs so the stud grid appears as cold stripes on the lining. The
vapour control layer
is incomplete and moisture accumulates in the stud cavities. Condensation appears on the lining in winter, and the actual heating demand is two to three times the theoretical.
